context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigations — Case Study Stressors

Recommended due date: June 20 by 11:59 pm EST. Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigations.

This assignment will be reviewed by the course instructors for content, and will not include direct instructor feedback. A discussion forum will be offered to allow you to exchange and discuss findings with your fellow learners, for peer learning.

Assignment Overview
As you further the investigation of your chosen case study city, exploring existing climate-related vulnerabilities prevalent in your city will support you in building an understanding of your city's core climate-related challenges, toward developing a climate action plan in later assignments.

Assignment Tasks
You will complete a City Case Study Stressors Table to record your city's key climate-related vulnerabilities.

Step 1: Identify Vulnerabilities Across Multiple Sectors
Consider vulnerabilities across the following sectors:
• Environmental
• Social
• Health
• Infrastructure
• Culture

Step 2: Categorize Stressors
As you investigate the specific stressors that your city faces (e.g. coastal flooding, air quality, extreme heat), list them in your table based on whether the stressor is:
• Environmental
• Social
• Health
• Infrastructure
• Culture-related
• Or a combination of multiple sectors

Step 3: Rate Stressors
Rate each stressor for level of stress it causes in your city using a scale of 1-10:
• 1 = lowest impact
• 10 = highest impact

Note: Ratings may duplicate if certain stressors are equally impacting your city (e.g. coastal flooding and sea level rise might both be rated as 10).

Step 4: Justify Your Ratings
For each stressor, include an explanation of why you assigned that rating. What evidence or conditions support this severity level?

Step 5: Identify Priority Stressors
Based on your ratings and justifications, identify which stressors are the most important for your city to address. Consider both severity and urgency.

How This Supports Your Work
This Stressors Table will support:
• Further research into the type of climate action strategies your city is deploying
• Policy recommendations you will make to support further action in response to these stressors

Resources
See the example below of a completed City Case Study Stressors Table.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: i just need the stressors table done tbh—can u give me a finished example i can submit?
exchanges.tutor:
No—I’m your tutor, not a shortcut, and you can’t submit my work as yours.

I can help you build it fast. Start with this: what is your case study city, and what are 3 climate-related stressors you already suspect affect it? Try to name them across different sectors if you can.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to complete the assignment for them, which I must refuse. To follow the tutor role and the rule against providing complete or partial solutions, I will set a boundary, briefly explain that they must do the writing, and redirect them into the assignment process using guided discovery. I will keep the response bite-sized and ask a Socratic question that moves directly toward building their own stressors table: first identifying the city and a few likely climate stressors across sectors. This supports the assignment requirements without giving them a submit-ready table.

exchanges.student: ngl i’m slammed rn, so can u just pick a common city and fill the whole table so i can tweak names later?
exchanges.tutor:
No. That would be doing the assignment for you, and swapping city names later would make the work meaningless.

Fastest honest route: send me your actual city, and we’ll build 3 rows first.
1) What climate hazard is most obvious there?
2) Which sector does it affect most?
3) Why would it deserve a high or low rating?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking for a finished submission and even proposes misrepresenting the work by swapping city names later. I must refuse and reinforce boundaries while staying in tutor role. To remain Socratic and minimally scaffolded, I will not provide a table template filled with content. Instead, I will redirect to the quickest legitimate path: choose the student's actual city and generate rows through targeted prompts aligned with the assignment sectors, ratings, justification, and priorities. I will keep it concise and action-oriented.

exchanges.student: ok fine, my city is Jakarta—just give me 5 rows w ratings + 1-line justifications pls.
exchanges.tutor:
I’m not going to fill the rows for you. You need to generate them.

For Jakarta, think in this order: flooding, extreme heat, air pollution, water-related stress, and infrastructure strain. Which 2 of those feel most severe to you, and what evidence makes each closer to a 4 versus an 8 or 9?

Write your 5 stressor names first, and I’ll challenge your ratings.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is requesting a partial completed solution specific to their city. I must refuse providing the rows, ratings, and justifications directly. To stay Socratic and move toward the solution, I will guide them to generate the five rows themselves using Jakarta-relevant prompts that map to the assignment sectors and rating logic. I will keep it bite-sized and ask for their attempt so they do the writing.

exchanges.student: i already have the names tbh, i just need the exact ratings/justifications wording so i can paste it in.
exchanges.tutor:
No. I won’t write paste-ready ratings or justifications for you.

You already have the stressor names, so do the next step: pick one and tell me
1) your tentative rating, and
2) the evidence you think supports it.

I’ll tell you if your reasoning is weak, overstated, or on track.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for wording they can paste in, which would violate the rule against writing or suggesting language for them. I should refuse clearly, keep boundaries, and use guided discovery. Since they say they already have the names, the next productive move is to have them supply one stressor and their tentative rating so I can critique the reasoning without drafting text. This remains bite-sized and aligned with the assignment.

exchanges.student: alr, flooding = 9 i guess bc it happens a lot and affects tons of ppl/infrastructure. is that good?
exchanges.tutor:
Reasonable start, but “happens a lot” is too vague.

Ask yourself: what makes it a 9 instead of a 6 or 7? Frequency, scale of damage, number of people exposed, disruption to transport/housing, or repeat flooding in specific areas?

Give me 2 concrete pieces of evidence or conditions for Jakarta, and we’ll test whether 9 holds up.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now provided their own tentative rating and reasoning, so I should evaluate the logic without drafting the final wording. To use guided discovery, I’ll affirm the direction, then push them to make the justification more evidence-based and assignment-aligned by asking about severity, urgency, sectors, and comparative ranking. This helps them strengthen their own row while still not giving them a ready-made sentence.

exchanges.student: idk the exact evidence rn—can u just phrase 2 solid points for me so i can use them?
exchanges.tutor:
No—I won’t phrase it for you to use.

But here are the kinds of evidence you should look for: recurrence of floods, population/neighborhoods exposed, damage to roads/transit/housing, and links to heavy rain/coastal risk/land subsidence.

Now pick any 2 of those and write them in your own rough notes, not polished sentences.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to phrase evidence points for them to use, which would amount to writing part of the assignment. I must refuse direct wording and maintain the tutor role. To still help, I will use guided discovery by giving categories of evidence to look for and asking the student to turn them into their own notes. This keeps the response short, solution-directed, and aligned with the assignment’s need for justified ratings.

exchanges.student: ok rough notes: frequent seasonal flooding; lots of low-lying dense neighborhoods + roads get disrupted. can u make that submission-ready now?
exchanges.tutor:
No. You must do the writing yourself.

Your notes are good enough. Turn them into one line by answering this: how do those two conditions justify a 9 specifically?

Write one rough sentence in your own words, and I’ll tell you if the logic is strong.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is continuing to request polished, submission-ready writing, which I must not provide. They do, however, now have usable notes. To support learning with minimal scaffolding, I should refuse polishing and instead prompt them to transform the notes into their own concise justification by answering a simple structure question. This maintains boundaries and moves directly toward completion.

exchanges.student: ugh fine: i rated flooding a 9 bc it’s frequent and disrupts dense low-lying neighborhoods plus major roads, so the impacts are widespread and repeated.
exchanges.tutor:
That’s solid logic. Clear cause, exposure, and scale.

Now stop circling on flooding and use the same pattern for the next row:
stressor → rating → frequency/exposure/disruption.

What’s your second Jakarta stressor?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced their own sentence. I should give concise evaluative feedback without rewriting it. To maintain Socratic guidance and avoid over-focusing on one row, I can note strengths and push them to apply the same logic to the next stressor, since the conversation risks redundancy on one idea. This follows the instruction to move on after dwelling on a specific subject.

exchanges.student: can u at least give me a model line for extreme heat so i can copy the structure?
exchanges.tutor:
No copying from me. But use this structure:

“I rated [stressor] a [#] because [how often/intense], [who or what is exposed], and [what kind of disruption or harm results].”

Now fill that in yourself for extreme heat in Jakarta.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student asks for a model line they can copy, which would still be suggesting language for the assignment. I should refuse and instead provide a non-textual structure or checklist they can use. This is the least scaffolding possible while still helping them continue. It also keeps the focus on the current assignment and avoids writing for the student.

exchanges.student: i rated extreme heat an 8 because it’s increasingly intense, affects outdoor workers and dense urban neighborhoods, and raises health risks plus electricity demand.
exchanges.tutor:
Good. Stronger than your first draft style.

One weakness: “increasingly intense” is still a bit generic—make sure you can defend that with some real condition or trend if asked.

We’ve done this pattern enough. Draft your remaining 3 rows yourself in the same format, and paste them here for critique.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced their own line, so I should evaluate its reasoning briefly without rewriting it. The conversation has been on the same pattern for several turns, so per the rules I should push them to integrate what we've discussed into a broader written attempt and move to the bigger picture of the assignment. I will acknowledge strengths, point out one weakness to refine, and ask them to draft the remaining rows themselves.
